title: College grads in ‘AI-proof’ careers like psychology and education are seeing negative returns on their degrees
description: As AI reshapes white-collar work, new research shows that some of the most popular graduate degrees are actually leaving holders worse off financially.
fullText:
There’s a boom in the economy: economics papers on the souring prospects of the recent college graduate in the AI-era economy of the 2020s. Harvard economists Lawrence Katz and Claudia Goldin found in September 2025 that the college wage premium remains, but has barely moved since 2000, while the San Francisco Fed attributed that stagnation primarily to less demand for those workers, in a working paper shortly afterward. The World Economic Forum found earlier this year that AI skills now command a 23% wage premium versus only 8% for a bachelor’s degree in isolation. Dallas Fed economist J. Scott Davis may have made the biggest splash in February 2026 with a paper that found AI is simultaneously reducing entry-level hiring and raising wages for experienced workers in the same AI-exposed occupations.

Why are engineering degrees showing surprisingly low returns?

Which graduate degrees offer negative financial returns?

How are AI skills changing traditional college wage premiums?

How does AI threaten white-collar job security prospects?

But what about the college grads that intentionally got degrees in supposedly “AI-proof” disciplines, like psychology or education?

A new report released by the Postsecondary Education and Economic Research Center maps out the estimated payoff of a graduate degree. When factoring in the costs of a graduate degree—tuition and fees—some degree holders are actually coming out the other end with negative returns. The worst returns are for psychology graduate degrees, with a -8% cost-adjusted return, or the estimated change in lifetime income after accounting for the cost of attendance.

The report also found that clinical psychology—a specialized branch of psychology—offers -5% cost-adjusted returns. Social work and curriculum and instruction degrees also offer negative returns, according to the study. Other popular degrees, such as computer science, yield only a 6% return after adjusting for costs.

“If you’re thinking about graduate school, you want to get some information about what the earnings potential is coming out of the degree as well as the kinds of occupations and jobs it leads to,” Joseph G. Altonji, a professor of economics at Yale and co-author of the study, told Fortune.

Over the years, more and more students have hedged their bets on a graduate degree to boost their salaries. The percentage of Americans with a graduate degree grew from 31% in 1993 to 42% in 2022, according to the U.S. Census Bureau. But as AI threatens the future of white-collar work, Gen Z, the generation just entering the workforce, is being forced to break with traditional work norms as the technology sparks a white-collar reckoning.

Research from Anthropic last month revealed that AI is theoretically capable of performing the majority of tasks in white-collar fields, such as engineering, law, and business and finance. As the Census suggests, many are still turning to the post-graduate degree (but a growing number are also ditching college altogether). Yet even as AI threatens to take jobs, some of the roles considered relatively safe from automation offer little in the way of job security.

title: GIRLS BB: Winamac's Kroft headlines All-Loganland team
description: Kandace Kroft broke multiple school records for the Winamac girls basketball team this season. She broke program records in career assists (363), career 3-pointers (222), single-season 3-pointers (97) and single-game 3-pointers (eight). The 5-foot-7 senior guard will graduate as Winamac’s fourth all-time leading scorer in program history with 1,214 points, joining a 1,000-point club that ...
fullText:
Kandace Kroft broke multiple school records for the Winamac girls basketball team this season.

She broke program records in career assists (363), career 3-pointers (222), single-season 3-pointers (97) and single-game 3-pointers (eight).

The 5-foot-7 senior guard will graduate as Winamac’s fourth all-time leading scorer in program history with 1,214 points, joining a 1,000-point club that includes Tiffany Crist (1993 grad, 1,374 points), Kara Crawford (2007, 1,245), Erika Redweik (2009, 1,226), Kroft (2026, 1,214), Stephanie Shorter (2014, 1,146), Shallon Bowden (1999, 1,112) and Jordan Brandt (2010, 1,108).

Kroft averaged 18.8 ppg, 6.5 rpg, 5.1 apg and 3.2 spg for a Winamac team that went 19-7. She is the Pharos-Tribune’s Loganland Player of the Year.

“I thought we had a really good season,” Kroft said. “We had a tough ending, but I was glad that we made it as far as we did because I think we struggled a little towards the end of the season. So making it to the sectional championship, it was a huge accomplishment. I’m just excited to continue to see the team improve. It was just the first four years under Coach Stesiak, so I know they’re gonna do great things in the future.”

Winamac coach Tony Stesiak took the Winamac job four years ago as Kroft was entering high school. The Warriors went 4-20 the first year, 14-10 in each of the next two years and 19-7 this year.

“She has had a tremendous career, a baptism by fire from her freshman year being the starting point guard from Day One,” Stesiak said. “We all had some growing pains that first year. And just to see her evolution, maturity and development through four years has been special.”

Stesiak previously coached the Rochester girls for 17 seasons and won a Class 2A state title in 2004 and a Class 3A regional title in 2015.

He said Kroft is up there among the best players he’s coached.

“She’s one of the best players I’ve ever coached and one of the toughest competitors, high basketball IQ, is all about winning,” he said. “That’s the thing that stuck out. Everything that she does is about trying to be the best she can be and the best team that we could be. She’s not necessarily a rah-rah type leader, but if you paid attention, as I always would tell especially this year’s freshmen, I hope you’re paying attention to how she does things because this is how you get to the as good as she is.”

Kroft will continue her career at Franklin College. The Grizzlies went 21-7 and finished runner-up in the HCAC this season.

“I’m really excited. I had a visit in early October and I’ve talked to the coaches and realized this is probably the place for me,” Kroft said. “They offer so many opportunities without basketball and obviously I’m not going to probably play basketball after college, so it was good to see the opportunities they would offer me after college. But I’ve also met some of the girls on the team and I’m just excited to jump into that chapter.”

Kroft also excels in the classroom. She has a weighted GPA of 4.048 and is ranked seventh in her senior class. She plans on studying business and marketing at Franklin.

Kroft is not the only great shooter in her family. The Winamac 3-point records she broke this year were originally set by her older sister, Kingsley, who she just missed playing alongside as Kingsley graduated four years ago as Kandace was about to enter high school.

“I think being able to watch her growing up made me want to get the records even more,” Kandace said. “But she taught me so much and seeing how good of a 3-point shooter that she was, I think it just motivated me to be better as it does sometimes in the family. But she was very supportive through everything, so I was just glad to have her there cheering for me.”

Their father, Kole Kroft, was the Winamac girls coach for five seasons following Jim Swaney and Jeff Wagner’s run as coach. Kole Kroft took a new day job four years ago and stepped aside, and Stesiak was hired.

Kandace Kroft broke Winamac school records and helped keep the program going strong over the last four years.

“She certainly left her mark on the Winamac Lady Warrior basketball program, which is tough to do considering all the great players that have come through Winamac,” Stesiak said. “I’ve coached against most of them, she’s the first one I’ve coached. She’s left her mark in the record books, but to see her development over four years and what she’s meant to our team and our program has truly been special. And like I told her, the best compliment I can give her is it’s gonna be hard for me to give No. 3 to anybody as long as I’m here just because of all the things that she’s meant to the program. I don’t know if retire the number is the right way to say, but it’s been an honor to be her coach. She’s just truly a special player and special person and I’m just honored to be able to say I was her coach at one point.”

The following is the rest of the 2026 All-Loganland girls basketball team:

The 5-foot-6 junior guard was a stat-sheet stuffer all season for the Comets. She averaged 16.5 ppg, 8.1 rpg, 3.8 apg and 3.7 spg and has already eclipsed the 1,000-point milestone with one full season left to play.

“I thought she showed tremendous growth this year as a player on the court,” Caston coach Josh Douglass said. “She upped her scoring average, upped her rebound average, upped to her assist average, upped her steals average. She took it to another level this year for herself on the court, showed great leadership on the court and did a lot for us. But what really impressed me was I think she had 27 assists last year and she ended the year with 90 this year. So finding teammates and teammates knocking down open shots which helped us become a better overall team this last year.”

The Comets went 11-13 and are not very far away from being much better. They’ll graduate just 0.5 ppg.

“We lost six games by five points or less,” coach Douglass said. “I mean, that’s almost a 17-win season.”

Cass County’s Lilly Scholarship winner is also gifted and talented on the basketball court. The 5-7 senior guard averaged 12.8 ppg, 7.1 rpg and 4.0 apg for the Panthers.

The Panthers went 10-16 and finished runner-up in their sectional with a 33-32 loss to West Central in the championship game.

“She just did everything we needed her to do,” Pioneer coach David McWherter said. “Relentless defender, improved on rebounding and that’s even with Liz (Kennell) on the court who averaged about 10 rebounds a game. Mia just did a great job for us all the way around. Great leadership — her leadership, her value to our team this year was just immeasurable. She put up 30 points against Winamac when they face guarded her most of the game. She went 8 for 14 from 3 that game, just an unbelievable game for her. And I think she really probably played her best game of the year might’ve been the Lewis Cass game that we lost. She had a double-double of 16 points and 15 rebounds. All season long she just carried us.”

The Kings’ track and cross country star also shined on the basketball court, averaging 11.2 ppg and 2.4 spg.

She’ll graduate as Cass’ sixth all-time leading scorer. The list includes Betsy Bowser (2003, 1,438), Angie Cree (1991, 1,434), Sharon Schreyer (1977, 1,163), Kendal Johnson (2022, 1,143), Savanna Thomson (2017, 1,099) and Griffin (2026, 1,062).

“She shot the ball extremely well. She had 45 3s on the season,” Cass coach Kyle Amor said. “She was four 3s away from the school record for 3s in a season, so just an incredible year. And she didn’t play in a game and a half because of her hand. At the holiday tournament she broke her hand — at least I think it was broke — and she missed a couple games because of that. And then the next three after she came back she barely shot a 3. So to still get her 1,000 and to still have that kind of offensive production standing all of 5-foot-1 I think is remarkable.

“Of course her athleticism and her speed make her someone hard to guard. And probably she’s the type of kid that a lot of teams zoned us because her foot speed getting past the defender was so good. But she’s just a such a good, coachable kid. If I’m talking to the locker room or in a huddle, her eyes are locked on me. She’s nodding. She’s just a coach’s dream that never gives you an issue, super competitive. You get a couple Aftin Griffins a lifetime, I feel like. And I was glad to be able to coach her.”

The Kings went 20-4, posted their best regular season in program history and had a last-second loss in double-overtime in their sectional. They were coming off back-to-back sectional titles and a semi-state appearance in 2024.

Griffin will run cross country and track at IU Indianapolis.

Another lightning quick guard for the Lady Kings, the 5-6 senior guard averaged 10.0 ppg, 2.9 apg and 3.9 spg.

Amor said Hedrick was the Lady Kings’ top defender and all-around player overall.

“Anna is our best person to just bug the heck out of the ball,” he said. “She was four steals away from breaking our school record for steals in a season.

“She was incredible. She’s our second all-time in career assists, second all-time in career steals, just a special athlete. And I don’t think Aftin gets her 1,000 points without Anna Hedrick on the floor. She’s been my point guard since her freshman year and I lean pretty heavily on her for floor leadership. It was as she goes, we go kind of thing. She’s just a special kid. She won our MVP this year and deservingly so voted by her teammates. As much as people talk about how great Aftin has been, I think Anna has been just as good for these last four years.”

The 5-7 junior guard averaged 13.0 ppg, 6.9 rpg and 2.9 apg.

“She’s flown under the radar because and rightly so because Kroft got a lot of the headlines and put up the really big numbers,” Stesiak said, “but if you would’ve put Sadie on any other team in the area, I think she would’ve been their leading scorer. She would’ve had a lot of those accolades. And as I told Sadie, she’s right there knocking on the door of everything Kroft has done through their junior years. If you look at a lot of the statistical things, Sadie’s right there on pace and in a few cases a little bit ahead of where Kandace was as a junior. She’s that three-level scorer, she’s got a ton of athleticism and every year she’s kind of developed more to her game.

“Looking to next year, hopefully it’s more just a retool and not a rebuild. It may look different, but there’s some dynamic things that Popejoy can do with the ball in her hands that not a lot of other players can. And she’s not under the radar now. Coaches who’ve coached against her and our coaching staff really appreciate all that she’s done and all that she’s put into it so far. But sort of the keys to the team are hers now.”

The 5-9 senior forward averaged 11.6 ppg and 8.6 rpg.

“Iverson’s just a little bit of an undersized post player, a good athlete, maybe not your traditional post player in having a lot of different post moves, but she has a knack for rebounding and scoring,” Stesiak said. “She just missed the season rebounding record this year. I think she was about 20 away from that. Just her ability to finish on the block and that dynamic of Kroft, Popejoy and Iverson worked really well together because teams had to guard Iverson in the post.

“She could catch a dump down from Kroft or Popejoy, she can get on offensive rebound, she can catch and finish around the basket and then a little bit of that mid-range game. And then she was a great rebounder. So those three really sort of gave us a triple threat that were a big part of our success.”

The 5-7 sophomore averaged 9.3 ppg.

“Grace was another one this year who had a tremendous jump too,” coach Douglass said. “Her scoring average I think will be even higher next year. She led us in made 3-pointers. I think she had 41 this year. She shot the ball better, her percentage was better. At the end of the year we finally got her driving to the basket again and getting to the foul line. That’s gonna be the big key for her next year is to stay aggressive like that. But she’s another girl that was a freshman last year, sophomore this year and she made that jump on the court. Now it’s just getting the consistency from her from game to game. Looking forward to having a tremendous junior year out of her.”

The Berries’ 5-11 senior center averaged 8.1 ppg, 8.3 rpg and 2.9 bpg.

“Goldie continued to progress throughout her senior year,” Logan coach Chris Keisling said. “I’d say she was obviously the kid that we depended upon inside. She consistently was our best scorer. The last game of the season at the sectional kind of epitomized her progress as she ended up getting 20 of our 26 against Western, I think she ended up about six rebounds. And so that’s kind of what she meant to us. We focused on getting the ball inside to her, trying to take some of the pressure off of the guard play out front. And so she just did a nice job improving throughout the year.

“When we look at where she was at her freshman year to her senior year, it just was remarkable. Her footwork got so much better. Her post play as a result of just her improved footwork inside was just much better.”

The Kings’ 5-11 sophomore forward averaged 7.0 ppg and 4.0 rpg

“She was our most improved player,” Amor said. “I could barely get her on the floor at the varsity level her freshman year. And I thought skill-wise, she probably deserved some more time, just physically wasn’t ready and mentally wasn’t ready. And she was our sixth man our first few games this year and then just earned that starting spot. Then it got towards the end of the year where we didn’t want to have her off the floor. She’s a kid that I think our success will lean on her quite a bit these next couple years. But she’s got a supporting cast around her that I think that we we’re not gonna drop off now that these seniors graduated.”

The Panthers’ 5-8 junior guard averaged 6.5 ppg.

“Avery just stepped up and made some big shots for us at times,” McWherter said. “Last year she just did everything for us defensively and didn’t give us a whole lot of offense, but this year she gave us a lot more offense. She made the Logansport Savings Bank team, made the Kitchen Classic team, put up 26 points against Taylor, she had six 3s. She went like 8 for 14 from the field in that game also.

“Her biggest asset for us last year and probably still this year besides shooting the 3s this year was her defensive putting pressure on the ball and playing the top of our zone. We’re really looking forward to having her back for her senior year.”

The 5-9 sophomore averaged 6.4 ppg and 4.8 rpg.

“Allison led us in 3-point percentage and led us in free throw percentage on the year,” coach Douglass said. “Her confidence just grew, especially towards the end of the year. She finally started asserting herself and being aggressive when she had the ball. And watching her grow from last year and being healthy was her biggest thing. This year she had the shoulder surgery over the summer, had a little bit of issues there on and off at the beginning of the year. But once she got confidence back in that shoulder her shooting was tremendous for us. Her ability to go inside for us and score inside and that aggressiveness and strength that she has and just being a sophomore looking at her for next year too and the work that she’ll put in.”

Layer, a 5-3 junior guard, averaged 6.4 ppg and 2.8 spg.

McWherter said he was glad the softball standout came back out for basketball.

“Without her playing basketball this year, we don’t win 10 ballgames, we maybe win six and we don’t get to the sectional championship,” he said. “She just does an outstanding job. She’s kind of our motor. She goes full speed. Another kid that just is very tough, she’s probably got the biggest toughness and the most tenacity for us, and she just completely gives us a ton of effort and her helping with the ball handling responsibilities, taking pressure off of Mia, like I said, if we don’t have her our 10 wins drop down to five or six because she just took so much of the pressure off of Mia.”

The 5-8 sophomore averaged 6.2 ppg and 9.6 rpg.

“Liz is just an athlete,” McWherter said. “She had six double-doubles on the year, had 10 or more rebounds in five of our seven conference games. The big game of the year she had for us was at Oregon-Davis, she had 10 offensive rebounds. She’s just a complete athlete, led us in field goal percentage at 49% on the season, led us in rebounding, just shy of 10 rebounds a game. We’re really look forward to having her for two more years.”

Clem, a 5-8 sophomore, averaged 5.7 ppg, 8.9 rpg and 3 spg.

“Lily’s been a really dependable rebounder for us and defender inside, and she also led us in steals this year,” Keisling said. “She is just very active defensively. We really focused on trying to improve her point production. That’s an area that she hasn’t really been a kid that’s looked to score, but this year we tried to do what we could to improve her scoring output, and she did improve her scoring output and started to look more to score this year which we think will help her as we head into next. But her defense, her rebounding at about 10 a game and her leading us in steals this year, those are the kind of things that she really brings to the table consistently.”

title: Sauk Valley grad Tony Tompkins named an NACDA Athletic Director of the Year
description: Sauk Valley Community College graduate and former Rock Falls boys basketball assistant coach Tony Tompkins was recently recognized by the National Association of Collegiate Directors of Athletics. Tompkins, the athletic director at Johnson County Community College, was named a recipient of the NACDA’s Athletic Director of the Year award. Tompkins is one of four award recipients this year from ...
fullText:
Sauk Valley Community College graduate and former Rock Falls boys basketball assistant coach Tony Tompkins was recently recognized by the National Association of Collegiate Directors of Athletics. Tompkins, the athletic director at Johnson County Community College, was named a recipient of the NACDA’s Athletic Director of the Year award.

Tompkins is one of four award recipients this year from Junior/Community College institutions. In all, only 28 recipients were honored from more than a thousand institutions across NCAA, NAIA, junior college and community college classifications.

He will be recognized in conjunction with NACDA’s 61st Annual Convention at Mandalay Bay Resort in Las Vegas on Tuesday, June 9.

“To be recognized by NACDA and honored alongside so many outstanding athletic directors from across the country is truly special,” he said. “I’m grateful for the opportunity to represent Johnson County Community College and the incredible work our coaches, staff and student-athletes do every day.”

The award recognizes athletics directors who have shown administrative excellence over the course of the last year through their commitment and positive contributions to student-athletes, campuses and the surrounding communities.

Tompkins has served as JCCC’s AD since 2022. The college is located in Overland Park, Kan. He resides in Kansas City with his wife, Heidi. They have three kids, Brooke, Haley and Samuel.

Over his tenure, JCCC athletic teams have produced one national championship, three national runner-up finishes, nine top-5 finishes, 20 national tournament berths, 18 Region 6 titles, 17 conference championships and 25 of the possible 26 teams to date over his term have ended the year ranked in the final NJCAA Poll.

Individually under his leadership, JCCC teams have produced 31 NJCAA All-Americans, two national players of the year, three national coaches of the year, 145 All-Region 6 selections, 180 All-KJCCC performers, 13 conference coaches of the year, 26 NJCAA Performers of the Week and 240 KJCCC Performers of the Week awards.

Tompkins also previously served as athletic director at Kansas City Community College.

He played basketball at Sauk Valley, where he earned an associate degree in Arts degree in 1996. He was an assistant coach for the Rockets boys basketball team when it won state in 1999. Tompkins was a standout athlete at Ohio High School.

He finished his undergraduate work at Missouri Baptist University in St. Louis, graduating with a BS in Secondary Education with a special emphasis in History. He received teacher certification the following year and holds a Masters Degree in Organizational Management from the University of Phoenix in 2003.

He was an assistant women’s basketball coach and head men’s coach at Missouri Baptist University and was head men’s coach at Judson University. During his four years as head coach at Judson, Tompkins also served as interim athletic director and assistant athletic director.

title: North Haven dental student died after Bridgeport Hospital put him in 'fake ICU,' lawsuit says
description: A North Haven family is suing Bridgeport Hospital, alleging a series of medical failures led to the death of 26-year-old UConn dental student Conor Hylton.
fullText:
MILFORD - A North Haven family is accusing Bridgeport Hospital of a series of medical failures that led to the death of their son, a young dental student, after he was left effectively unattended in what their attorney calls a "fake ICU."

Conor Hylton, 26, died Aug. 15, 2024, after being treated at the Milford campus of Bridgeport Hospital, according to a medical malpractice lawsuit filed in state Superior Court in Bridgeport. Bridgeport Hospital is owned by Yale New Haven Hospital.

The lawsuit alleges Hylton was placed in the hospital's intensive care unit but allegedly without proper monitoring, without a doctor at his bedside and without basic life-saving precautions.

"It was a tragedy that definitely could have been avoided," said Joel Faxon of the Faxon Law Group, who is representing Hylton's estate. "The family is absolutely devastated. He was a good kid. He was a very good student. He was at UConn Dental School and parents are both dentists."

"Yale New Haven Health is aware of this lawsuit and is committed to providing the safest and highest quality of care possible, however, we are unable to comment on pending litigation," said Amanda Raus, media relations coordinator at Bridgeport Hospital.

The day before he died, Hylton walked into the emergency room at the former Milford Hospital complaining of abdominal pain and vomiting. Within hours, his condition worsened and he was diagnosed with pancreatitis, dehydration, metabolic acidosis and alcohol withdrawal, according to a medical analysis report on Hylton that was included in the lawsuit.

What happened next, his attorney alleges in the lawsuit, was a deadly breakdown in care.

The medical analysis stated the hospital campus uses a "tele-ICU," which means there were no ICU intensivists present on site when Hylton was a patient during the overnight hours.

Despite being in intensive care, no on-site physician assessed Hylton for hours, the lawsuit claims. The doctor assigned to oversee him, hospitalist Dr. Frances Demur, allegedly never saw him at all, even after being notified of his decline. Instead, care was partly handled through a "tele-ICU" system, where doctors monitor patients remotely.

Attorney Faxon said neither the family, nor anyone else, would not have consented to Hylton going into a tele-ICU.

"It's a fake ICU. It's not real because no patient would ever consent if they told ... they're not going to have a doctor in here. They're going to be on the tube," Faxon said.

As Hylton's condition worsened overnight, the lawsuit alleges basic medical protocols were ignored.

Required assessments for alcohol withdrawal were not performed, fluid monitoring was not done and no physician evaluated him despite his declining condition. His parents were never notified as his health deteriorated, according to the medical report and the lawsuit.

At 4:30 a.m., Hylton slid down in his bed, became unresponsive, showed seizure-like activity and began vomiting. His heart rate dropped, and a code was called. He was intubated but could not be resuscitated, according to the medical report. He died at 6:09 a.m.

The lawsuit alleges a lack of communication among providers, missing or delayed nursing documentation and confusion during the emergency response. In one instance, a provider called to perform a critical procedure could not immediately locate the ICU, contributing to delays in care. It also claims there were inaccurate entries in the medical record.

The lawsuit argues Hylton's death was preventable, pointing to a critical failure to protect his airway while he was being treated with powerful sedatives that affected his consciousness. As his condition worsened, the complaint says, his airway became unstable and was not properly managed.

This article originally published at North Haven dental student died after Bridgeport Hospital put him in 'fake ICU,' lawsuit says.

title: Former New Jersey Teacher Accused of Raping Teen Student Sobs in Court as Judge Denies Bail
description: A former New Jersey middle school teacher arrested last month for raping one of her students sobbed as a judge determined she would remain jailed until her case is resolved. In video footage of a Glou...
fullText:
A former New Jersey middle school teacher arrested last month for raping one of her students sobbed as a judge determined she would remain jailed until her case is resolved.

In video footage of a Gloucester County Court bail hearing, accessed by Us Weekly, Ashley Fisler can be seen covering her mouth with her hands in shock as Judge William Ziegler announced he feels the married former educator poses a risk to the community at large.

Fisler, 36, could then be seen wiping tears from her eyes and pouting as the judge spoke in her hearing on Wednesday, April 1. She was also seen rocking back and forth, comforting herself.

Fisler has been charged with six counts of first-degree sexual assault of a minor, one count of second-degree endangering the welfare of a child and one count of second-degree official misconduct.

Fisler was arrested after the victim reported the allegations to police on January 23. The victim, who is now an adult, said that they were sexually assaulted six times by Fisler while they were a student in her class at Orchard Valley Middle School.

Married Former Kansas Teacher Accused of Grooming and Having Unlawful Sexual Relations With Student, 17

He turned 14 in 2021 during the alleged sexual abuse.

Investigators said they obtained 7,500 pages of incriminating text messages between Fisler and the victim, which confirmed the sexual nature of their relationship.

She allegedly had sex with the student in her car and her classroom in the school.

Fisler allegedly maintained contact with the child after the rapes and asked him to talk about the sexual encounters he was having with girls his own age.

She also offered to buy him a sex toy and groom his private parts, according to the texts, police said.

In a message the teen sent on January 2, 2025, he told Fisler, “I have to try really hard to rebuild the things you broke inside of me. You destroyed things inside of me. You stripped me of my innocence.”

Fisler, who left teaching in 2023, has not entered pleas to the charges she faces.

Married HS Gym Teacher Arrested for Sexually Assaulting Student: ‘You Can’t Tell Anybody’

The New York Post reported her lawyer, Rocco Cipparone, argued that texts showed the alleged victim in this case was no “babe in the woods.”

“He’s not texting like a kid … his texts are as graphic and direct as any I’ve seen from an adult,” Cipparone said. “I’m not saying he’s not the age he is, but this is not a babe in the woods scenario that [the prosecutor] is creating from a risk and a danger perspective.”

She is due back in court on May 14.

Each first-degree charge she is facing carries a maximum of 20 years in prison, while she could face a maximum of 10 years in prison for each second-degree charge.

If you or someone you know has been sexually assaulted, contact the National Sexual Assault Hotline at 1-800-656-HOPE (4673).

title: Not One, But Two Teachers at the Same Arizona High School Reportedly Had an Inappropriate Relationship With the Same Student and Neither Has Been Arrested
description: Teachers at Centennial High School are reportedly facing potential felony charges after a police investigation into a complaint of having inappropriate relationships with the same student. Haley Beck,...
fullText:
Teachers at Centennial High School are reportedly facing potential felony charges after a police investigation into a complaint of having inappropriate relationships with the same student.

Haley Beck, a 27-year-old social studies teacher and soccer coach at Centennial High, and Angela Burlaka, a 47-year-old teacher with 26 years of experience at the school, were placed on administrative leave in late 2025. Burlaka was terminated in January. The Peoria Unified School District school board approved Beck’s termination in March. She has until April 8 to request a hearing on her termination.

Peoria Unified School Board voted to terminate Centennial High teacher
Haley Beck

“The respondent maintained her professional position and used her power to manipulate the complainant and to develop a relationship outside the professional boundaries between student and teacher” pic.twitter.com/Nt8tBwTRPu

— Heather Rooks The Peoria Mom (@ThePeoriaMom) March 28, 2026

“After a review of the investigator’s findings, the decision maker found that Ms. Beck groomed a student, which resulted in her having a sexual relationship with that student, and therefore recommended her termination,” said Danielle Airey, a district spokesperson.

Peoria Police claim that the teacher exchanged sexually explicit messages with the student, spent more than $600 on the student during several weeks, helped the student with homework assignments, allowed the student to use her car, and purchased marijuana and alcohol on the student’s behalf. The student’s mother told the investigators that the student and the teacher’s relationship began when the student was 17.

According to the police records, Beck called the situation “straight prostitution” and referred to the student as her “sugar baby.” The investigation into the situation determined whether money and gifts were exchanged in return for the sexual acts.

Burlaka is accused of sending the student explicit videos of herself while the student was still a minor.

The Maricopa County Attorney’s Office decided to charge Beck with the crime of inducing prostitution, as well as Burlaka, who is accused of aggravated luring of a minor for sexual exploitation. Still, the investigation has been returned to the police to investigate the situation further. As of the investigation, neither teacher has been arrested.

Centennial High School Principal Scott Hollabaugh told police he had received several complaints from students and staff about the situation involving Beck and the student.

Another school administrator said he had brought the situation to Hollabaugh’s attention, but the principal said the incident involving Beck and the student was not the first.

According to police records, unconfirmed witness reports suggest other students may have been involved in similar situations with the teachers.

“There has been repeated information that there are other students who are potential victims in this case,” the records state.

title: Ole Miss Student Blasts Oxford in Viral TikTok: ‘There’s Literally Nothing to Do’
description: A TikTok video by a University of Mississippi student detailing her reasons for leaving the school has gone viral, and people are weighing in on their opinions on the situation.… The post Ole Miss Stu...
fullText:
A TikTok video by a University of Mississippi student detailing her reasons for leaving the school has gone viral, and people are weighing in on their opinions on the situation.

Maggie Recco, who originally transferred to Ole Miss from Arizona before leaving for the University of Miami, posted a video explaining why she left Oxford, Mississippi, after attending there for a year.

Her grievances ranged from a lack of dining and shopping venues to a social scene she found to be phony.

“There’s literally nothing to do in Oxford,” Recco said in the video. “There’s no Target. The only grocery store is Walmart, which you can’t go to without running into everyone you know.”

She also complained about a lack of late-night dining options, saying that after 10 p.m., the only places open were fast-food restaurants, and drive-through wait times were over an hour.

She also complained about the nightlife in Oxford, saying that many venues close as early as 12:30 a.m. and have high cover charges.

“You’re paying 20 bucks minimum to get into the bar,” she said in the video, adding, “And on game days, it’s a lot higher than that. One time, it was 200 bucks to get into the bar.”

She told a particular story about her and her friends looking for a place to go at closing time.

“We got to this bar, they were asking 80 bucks to get in, with the bar closing in 30 minutes.”

She also complained about police activity in the Oxford nightlife scene, saying they were patrolling in uniforms on horseback, as well as undercover agents from alcohol control who raided venues regularly.

“The square is flooded with police,” she said in the video, adding, “Police on horses are just riding around, literally looking for underage students.”

Transportation issues were another source of frustration. While the airport was an hour and 20 minutes away in Memphis, Recco said that rideshare services were too costly. “If you’re gonna get an Uber, it’s gonna be like $150 just to go to the airport,” she said.

Recco also complained about what she said was an entitled attitude among many of her classmates. “Everyone’s just a little fake and spoiled and not used to being told no,” she said. “You’d think, because it’s a Southern school, but a lot of it feels fake hospitality.”

Parking tickets were another source of frustration. “I’ve gotten over $1,000 in tickets,” she said, adding that parking personnel took joy in issuing tickets. “They actually hate you.”

Recco concluded by describing her thoughts on the school. “The school is a summer camp for people who are 19 to 23,” she said. “It’s not real. It’s fake.”

Recco concluded the video by indicating she had been warned about the school. “They were right,” she said. “And now I’m gonna move to Miami, so I’ll be in a city I like.”

The video elicited a variety of responses, some supportive and others quite negative. One commenter said she had not made any friends and was having trouble adjusting to college. “Just had no friends,” she said.

Another commenter questioned the announcement’s public nature. “It’s your life decision,” the commenter wrote. “Log off the apps and start doing something important.”

A third commenter took a more sarcastic approach to Recco’s educational path, which took her to three different schools, saying she had to run the “hoe gauntlet.” This comment also generated a lot of interest on the site.

Do you think Maggie’s reasons are valid?

The post Ole Miss Student Blasts Oxford in Viral TikTok: ‘There’s Literally Nothing to Do’ appeared first on Where Is The Buzz | Breaking News, Entertainment, Exclusive Interviews & More.

The shooting happened at Hill Country College Preparatory High School in Bulverde, the Comal County Sheriff’s Office said in a social media post. No other injuries were reported.

The teacher was taken to a San Antonio hospital, but the sheriff’s office did not have an update on her condition. The 15-year-old male student died on the scene, the sheriff’s office said.

A spokesperson for the sheriff’s office told The Associated Press that the student died from a self-inflicted gunshot.

The school was placed on lockdown, and students were taken to a middle school to be reunited with parents, the sheriff’s office said.

The high school, which is part of the Comal Independent School District, focuses on academics and skills to prepare students for college, according to the district's website. Its curriculum is centered on science, technology, engineering, arts and math, known as STEAM, with electives that include cybersecurity and engineering.

title: Form 8K SLM STUDENT LOAN TRUST 2006-7 For: 30 March
UEdu – Student writing mental health signals with 40 psycholinguistic features
description: UEDU: Mental health distress signal detection in student writing using psycholinguistic NLP. 40 features, XGBoost, Glass Box explainable engine. 100% local, no cloud AI needed. - harold-wang-dev/uedu
fullText:
Open-source NLP toolkit for detecting mental health distress signals in student writing using psycholinguistic features. 100% local, privacy-first, no cloud AI needed.

Understand. Evaluate. Describe. Uplift.

UEDU is an open-source Python library for mental health signal detection in student writing. It extracts 40 psycholinguistic features (emotion, cognition, social dynamics, linguistic patterns) and uses a pre-trained XGBoost classifier to identify distress signals -- with a deterministic, explainable "Glass Box" report engine grounded in MHFA ALGEE and BC ERASE clinical protocols.

All processing runs locally. Zero data leaves the machine. Designed for educators, school counselors, and mental health researchers worldwide. Originally developed and tested in British Columbia, Canada (FOIPPA-compliant).

Keywords: mental health NLP, student wellbeing, psycholinguistic analysis, suicide detection, text classification, XGBoost, explainable AI, school counseling, SHAP, feature engineering, distress detection, education technology

Teachers read hundreds of student assignments. Some contain distress signals -- hopelessness, self-harm language, social withdrawal -- buried in ordinary schoolwork. These signals are easy to miss at scale, and existing tools either require cloud processing (violating student privacy laws) or lack clinical grounding.

UEDU solves this: a fully local, deterministic pipeline that flags concerning writing patterns and gives teachers actionable, protocol-backed guidance.

Pre-trained models (M5.pkl, M6.pkl) are included. No dataset download required for inference.

Every step is deterministic. Same input always produces the same output. No network calls.

Prerequisites: Python 3.10+, pip

spaCy is optional. Pass use_spacy=False to skip POS-based temporal features (faster, slightly less accurate).

All features are deterministic word-ratio calculations. No randomness, no model inference.

Trained and evaluated on 231,938 Reddit posts (5-fold cross-validation):

M5 is the recommended model for local deployment (40 features, no TF-IDF vocabulary dependency).

We audited the model on 24,728 ASAP student essays across ELL status, race/ethnicity, and gender:

The model does not penalize students for being ESL learners. See src/fairness/metrics.py for the full audit methodology.

Training data is not included due to privacy and licensing. Download separately if you want to retrain:

You do not need any dataset to run inference. The pre-trained M5 and M6 models are included.

UEDU was developed by Harold W., a Grade 11 student in British Columbia, Canada, as a Health Sciences research project for the Greater Vancouver Regional Science Fair (GVRSF 2026).

The goal is to give educators a transparent, privacy-respecting tool for early identification of student mental health concerns, so that no student's silent distress goes unnoticed.

title: Authorities find missing college student from Missouri dead in Madison County
description: “We ask that you keep her family in your thoughts during this difficult time,” St. Peter’s police stated.
fullText:
A missing 21-year-old college student whose vehicle was abandoned in Madison County on Thursday has been found dead.

Police in St. Peters, Missouri, where the woman’s family lived and where she was last seen by family members, posted a brief statement on Saturday afternoon on Facebook.

“Melissa Oelke has been located deceased in Madison County,” it states. “This remains an active investigation, but we do not believe there is a threat to the public. We ask that you keep her family in your thoughts during this difficult time. Thank you to everyone who shared this post and offered your prayers.”

The statement didn’t give a cause of death.

Authorities had been searching for Oelke, who apparently left home about 7:30 a.m. Thursday, leaving her cellphone behind.

Oelke visited a Target store near her home that morning, then a Schnucks store in Chesterfield, Missouri, and then a Walgreens in Florissant, Missouri, according to her father, Gerald Oelke.

St. Jacob Police received a call about 12:30 p.m. on Thursday reporting an abandoned vehicle on Keck Road, about a mile east of Illinois 4. Interstate 70 is about 3 miles away.

Melissa Oelke attended the College of Creative Studies, a private art and design school in Detroit, Michigan, her father said. He noted that she had completed her midterms, seemed stressed and decided to extend her spring break and take care of her mental health.

Gerald Oelke also had speculated that perhaps his daughter was trying to return to school.

“Melissa has several medical conditions that require attention, and there is concern for her well‑being,” the St. Peters Police Department stated in a previous news release.

The search for Oelke had included Illinois Emergency Management Agency personnel flying drones over a wooded area near farm fields; Madison County Sheriff’s Office deputies riding all-terrain vehicles; a K-9 and his handler working the area; and an Illinois State Police plane circling overhead.

title: Pounders' selflessness part of historic season
description: Parker Pounders is team-first. Ever since he made the Booneville soccer team in seventh grade following time as a student manager, he has put the team’s needs ahead of his own. However, after a while, his individual accomplishments became something one had to notice. After leading the Blue Devils on a historic run, Pounders is the Daily Journal’s 2025-26 Boys Soccer Player of the Year. “I’m ...
fullText:
Ever since he made the Booneville soccer team in seventh grade following time as a student manager, he has put the team’s needs ahead of his own. However, after a while, his individual accomplishments became something one had to notice.

After leading the Blue Devils on a historic run, Pounders is the Daily Journal’s 2025-26 Boys Soccer Player of the Year.

“I’m definitely really excited,” he said. “It was kind of surprising at first, but I think it's really a testament to how hard our team has worked, and not just even an individual award, but how we've had success as a team this year, too.”

Pounders led the Blue Devils with 43 goals and was tied for the team lead with 12 assists. His effort was a big reason for Booneville’s best season in the program’s seven-year history.

The Blue Devils finished 21-3, which included first place in Class I Region 1 and the team’s first ever North half final appearance.

“He's put in a ton of work in getting to the point where he is, and nobody deserves it more than him,” Booneville coach Bart Greenhaw said. “He loves the sport, and he's been out here, a manager since sixth grade all the way, and year after year, he's gotten better and better and improved his game. We're just certainly excited he got this opportunity.”

Pounders played in 19 games and scored three or more goals in nine of them. His unique knack for scoring, and offensive production in general, made him a standout player. However, it wasn’t the scoring he was focused on.

“I think I kind of had to go into the games thinking, not how many I can score, but just really how many games we can win, and what I have to do for us to win,” Pounders said. “But definitely my teammates worked really hard in the offseason and improved. So that kind of opened up a lot of opportunities.”

Those opportunities also came as a result of hard work, which he’s put in ever since he joined the team.

“He's always been one that's here early and stays late. Now we have this new field that he can come to any time he wants,” Greenhaw said. “He's played club growing up. He's just put in a lot of time and effort.”

title: Suburban Detroit school settles lawsuit with Palestinian student over Pledge of Allegiance dispute
description: A suburban Detroit school district has agreed to give First Amendment training to staff to settle a lawsuit by a teenager who said a teacher humiliated her for refusing to stand for the Pledge of Alle...
fullText:
DETROIT (AP) — A suburban Detroit school district has agreed to give First Amendment training to staff to settle a lawsuit by a teenager who said a teacher humiliated her for refusing to stand for the Pledge of Allegiance in protest of U.S. support of Israel's war in Gaza.

The agreement with Danielle Khalaf and her father also includes a $10,000 payment by an insurance company on behalf of the teacher, according to a court filing.

The Plymouth-Canton district did not admit liability. But Superintendent Monica Merritt praised Danielle for “showing courage and speaking up about the incident.”

“Our mission is to foster a school environment that is safe, respectful and welcoming for all,” Merritt said Friday.

Danielle, whose family is of Palestinian descent, declined to recite the pledge at her school over three days in January 2025. The lawsuit says her teacher admonished her and told her she was being disrespectful.

“Since you live in this country and enjoy its freedom, if you don’t like it, you should go back to your country,” the teacher said, according to the lawsuit.

Danielle suffered emotional injuries, including nightmares and strained friendships, the lawsuit said.

“It was terrifying at times, scary to face a teacher and overwhelming with the attention that came with the publicity. But it taught me the importance of speaking up for what I believe is right,” Danielle said Thursday in a statement released by the American Civil Liberties Union and Arab American Civil Rights League.

Michigan has more than 300,000 residents of Middle Eastern or North African descent, second in the U.S. behind California, according to the Census Bureau.

The school district will remove anything from Danielle's file that suggests her actions violated school policy, according to the settlement.

title: Brigham Young University to make Honor Code exception for student who filed lawsuit alleging religious discrimination
description: Less than a week after a Brigham Young University student filed a lawsuit against the school, claiming male haircut restrictions discriminated against his religious beliefs, Brigham Young University h...
fullText:
PROVO, Utah (ABC4) — Less than a week after a Brigham Young University student filed a lawsuit against the school, claiming male haircut restrictions discriminated against his religious beliefs, Brigham Young University has confirmed that they will be making an exception for him.

Brigham Young University (BYU) requires all students to sign and adhere to their Honor Code, which includes standards on personal grooming students. Part of the Honor Code reads, “Men’s hair should be neatly trimmed” and that their hair “should be cut short”.

On March 18, Jerrhan First Charger filed a lawsuit against Brigham Young University (BYU). First Charger is a member of a Native American tribe and recently started attending BYU. He is also on the school’s dance team and had to sign the honor code in order to participate in it.

How to make ABC4 your preferred news source on Google

First Charger requested a religious accommodation as cutting his hair would violate his religious beliefs, but BYU allegedly refused to provide any accommodation.

He alleges that BYU has ordered him to cut his hair or face suspension from the school, despite the fact that his long hair “is a sacred expression of his religious faith and tribal identity”, according to the lawsuit.

“The ultimatum forces Plaintiff to choose between violating his religious beliefs and cultural identity or losing his educational opportunities and athletic participation.” The lawsuit reads. “The threat of suspension and disciplinary action has caused Plaintiff severe emotional distress and anxiety.”

According to the lawsuit, First Charger has also sent several letters and emails to BYU officials asking them to make a religious exception. The Union of British Columbia Indian Chiefs also sent a letter on his behalf that partially read, “There is already a long history of expected assimilation and destruction of Indigenous cultures through mandates and discriminatory practices.”

First Charger also brought up an exception made for a previous BYU student, Michael Rafael Williamson Tabango, who is a member of the indigenous Kichwa Nation and whose long hair is a part of his cultural identity, like First Charger.

Previous Coverage: BYU student petitioning Honor Code to preserve his native identity

Additionally, in the letters sent to BYU, First Charger pointed out that members of the football team had long hair but did not face suspension. He alleges that this could be due to favoritism, and notes that he maintains a neat and tidy appearance even with long hair.

On March 18, after BYU reportedly did not respond to First Charger’s letters, he filed a lawsuit against BYU, asking that the court prevents the school from suspending or disciplining him, or enforcing its hair-length policy against him.

Additionally, First Charger is asking the court to declare that BYU has violated federal civil rights laws and the court to require BYU to pay compensatory or punitive damages and award attorney’ fees and other relief as deemed proper.

BYU has since confirmed that they will be making an exception for First Charger, meaning that he will not have to cut his hair in order to remain at school and participate in the dance team.

A spokesperson from Brigham Young University provided a joint statement on behalf of the school and First Charger’s legal team. The statement reads:

“Brigham Young University and Jerrhan First Charger have reached a resolution in this matter. Mr. First Charger will continue his studies at BYU and his participation in the BYU Living Legends Dance Team. Both parties are grateful for the opportunity to resolve this matter.

The university’s Honor Code, including its dress and grooming standards, reflects BYU’s mission and remains in effect. We also recognize on a case-by-case basis there might be rare exceptions.”

title: New Jersey middle school teacher charged with child sexual assault after alleged relationship with student
description: A former New Jersey middle school teacher faces multiple charges after allegedly engaging in a sexual relationship with a student in 2021.
fullText:
A former middle school teacher in New Jersey was arrested on Thursday following allegations she had had a sexual relationship with a student.

Ashley Fisler, 36, of Washington Township in Gloucester County, was charged with six counts of first-degree sexual assault of a minor, one count of second-degree endangering the welfare of a child and one count of second-degree official misconduct, according to Fox 29.

Each first-degree charge carries a maximum of 20 years in prison, and each second-degree charge carries a maximum of 10 years.

New Jersey Teacher Who Slept With Students At Family Bagel Shop Learns Prison Sentence

The arrest of the former teacher comes after the victim, who is now an adult, reported the details to police earlier this year. The victim was a student in Fisler's class at the time of the sexual relationship.

Fisler allegedly engaged in a sexual relationship with a student in 2021 while she was a teacher at Orchard Valley Middle School, the outlet reported.

The victim described to police multiple sexual encounters in Fisler’s vehicle and in her classroom, according to Fox 29.

Investigators later discovered text messages between Fisler and the victim that allegedly confirmed the sexual nature of their relationship, the outlet reported.

West Virginia Teacher Fired After Arrest On Child Sex Abuse Charge

Fisler taught social studies in the Washington Township school district from 2014 until June 2023, according to an online resume.

She is no longer employed as a teacher in Washington Township or in any other district, according to officials.
